title: Voo Sound Practice for Vagal Reset
dek: *The voo sound is a deceptively simple vagal toning exercise that uses your own voice as an instrument to recalibrate your autonomic nervous system. When anger lives unprocessed in your body, this humming practice offers a direct physiological pathway back to safety and presence, one breath at a time.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Voo sound practice activates the vagus nerve through sustained low-frequency vibration, shifting you from fight-or-flight into rest-and-digest physiology. The technique uses a controlled exhale with an open "voo" tone to massage the nerve pathways along your throat, chest, and diaphragm. Regular practice rewires your nervous system's baseline, making emotional regulation and anger processing more accessible over time.

She sat in the driver's seat with the engine off, hands still gripping the wheel. The meeting had ended twenty minutes ago. Her jaw was set, her shoulders tight as bridge cables, and somewhere behind her sternum sat a hot stone she couldn't name. Not anger, she told herself. Just stress. Just a bad day. Just the way things are. Her breath was shallow, high in her chest, and she scrolled her phone without reading a single word. The body keeps the score, even when the mind refuses to count. ## What the Vagus Nerve Actually Does in Your Anger Response The vagus nerve is the longest cranial nerve in your body, wandering from your brainstem down through your face, throat, heart, lungs, and digestive organs. It's the primary highway of your parasympathetic nervous system, the branch responsible for rest, digestion, social connection, and emotional regulation. When your vagus nerve has good tone (meaning it responds flexibly to stress and recovers quickly), you can move through anger without getting stuck in it. Poor vagal tone, on the other hand, leaves you trapped in sympathetic arousal. Your heart rate stays elevated. Your digestion shuts down. Your thinking narrows. According to the APA's research summary on anger management, chronic anger correlates with cardiovascular disease, weakened immune function, and disrupted social relationships. What the research often misses is the physiological mechanism underneath: a nervous system that can't downregulate because the vagal brake is weak. Soraya Chemaly's work on women and anger highlights how cultural suppression of anger creates a physiological double bind. When you're taught that your anger is inappropriate or dangerous, you don't stop feeling it. You just stop recognizing it. The charge remains in your nervous system, circulating without outlet, and your vagus nerve learns to stay in a state of chronic defensiveness. ## How Sound Vibration Directly Stimulates Vagal Pathways The vagus nerve has branches that pass directly alongside your vocal cords, through the muscles of your throat, and down into the tissues around your heart and diaphragm. When you create sustained low-frequency sound, the vibration mechanically stimulates these nerve fibers. It's not metaphor. It's anatomy. The voo sound specifically uses a low, open vowel that creates maximal resonance in your chest cavity. Unlike a hum (which vibrates primarily in your head and sinuses) or an om (which rises in pitch), voo stays low and forward. You can feel it rumble in your sternum, your ribs, the base of your throat. That sensation is the vagus nerve receiving direct mechanical input. In my own practice, I've noticed that clients who resist sitting meditation or find breathwork too activating will often respond to voo sound. It gives the thinking mind something neutral to focus on while the body does the deeper work. The sound becomes a bridge between conscious intention and autonomic recalibration. ## The Basic Voo Sound Technique Sit or stand comfortably. Take a full breath in through your nose, letting your belly expand. On the exhale, release a sustained "voo" sound, as if you're saying the word "food" but holding the vowel indefinitely. Let the pitch be low and natural, somewhere in the lower third of your vocal range. Don't force it into a growl, but don't let it rise into your head voice either. The exhale should be slow and controlled, emptying your lungs over eight to twelve seconds. At the end, pause for a moment in the emptiness before inhaling again. Repeat for five to ten cycles. Your body will find its rhythm. You might feel tingling in your hands, warmth in your chest, or a sudden yawn. These are signs of parasympathetic activation. If you're working with unfelt anger, the sound might bring up emotion. Let it. Karla McLaren's research on emotional flow emphasizes that anger, when given appropriate expression, moves through the body in waves. Voo sound creates a container for that movement. You're not performing anger or venting it at someone. You're giving your nervous system permission to complete the stress cycle that anger initiated. ## Why Low Frequency Matters More Than Volume You don't need to be loud. In fact, volume often works against the practice. When you push sound too hard, you engage your shoulders, neck, and jaw, muscles that are already holding tension. The goal is vibration, not projection. Think of it as an internal massage, not a performance. The frequency range that most effectively stimulates vagal tone is between 80 and 150 Hz. For most people, a relaxed voo sound naturally falls into this range. You can verify this with a free tuner app if you're curious, but your body will tell you when you've found the sweet spot. It feels like a gentle buzz behind your breastbone, a hum that seems to settle your heart rate without effort. Over the years I've seen people try to "do it right" by mimicking a recording or matching someone else's pitch. That misses the point. Your vagus nerve responds to your voice, in your body, at your natural frequency. Trust the sensation more than the sound. ## Pairing Voo Sound with Somatic Awareness Peter Levine's work on trauma and the nervous system teaches that regulation happens through pendulation, the gentle oscillation between activation and settling. Voo sound practice is most effective when you pair it with body awareness, noticing where you feel the vibration and where you feel resistance. Before you begin, scan your body. Notice where you're holding tension. Common spots: the hinge of your jaw, the space between your shoulder blades, the solar plexus, the pelvic floor. As you sound, imagine the vibration moving into those areas. You're not trying to force relaxation. You're offering your nervous system new information, a somatic reminder that it's safe to let go. Between rounds, pause and notice what's shifted. Has your breath deepened? Have your shoulders dropped? Is there a softness in your belly that wasn't there before? These micro-changes are the nervous system learning a new baseline. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to this principle: sustainable change happens in the body first, through repeated small experiences of safety. ## Building a Daily Practice (and What Gets in the Way) Start with three minutes. Seriously. Most people abandon practices because they set unsustainable goals. Three minutes of voo sound before coffee, or in your car before you walk into work, or as a boundary between your workday and your evening is enough to begin shifting your vagal tone. You'll encounter resistance. The voice in your head that says this is silly, or that you're doing it wrong, or that you don't have time. That voice is often the same mechanism that keeps your anger unfelt. It's the inner critic that learned to shut down your authentic expression. Notice it, and sound anyway. If you live with other people and feel self-conscious, try it in the shower. The white noise of the water masks the sound, and the steam opens your throat. Or do it in your car with the windows up. The enclosed space amplifies the vibration, and no one can hear you. You might also explore the herbal support in [our herbal cabinet](/herbs), where adaptogens like holy basil and ashwagandha can support your nervous system's capacity to regulate. [Source Naturals Theanine Serene with Holy Basil](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0014H3B3G?tag=spankyspinola-20) combines calming amino acids with vagal-supportive herbs, creating a foundation that makes practices like voo sound more accessible. ## The Difference Between Vagal Toning and Emotional Bypassing There's a risk here worth naming. Voo sound is not a substitute for feeling your anger. It's not a technique to make difficult emotions go away. It's a tool to help your nervous system stay present with what's actually happening. If you're using the practice to avoid a necessary confrontation, or to numb yourself to an injustice that requires action, you're not toning your vagus nerve. You're just training yourself to dissociate more efficiently. The litmus test is this: after a session of voo sound, do you feel more capable of naming what you're angry about, or less? Do you have more access to your boundaries, or less? True vagal toning increases your window of tolerance. It gives you the physiological stability to feel your anger without being consumed by it, and to act from that anger with clarity rather than reactivity. If you're uncertain whether you're processing anger or bypassing it, [the assessments hub](/assessments) offers frameworks to help you discern the difference. ## Complementary Practices and Physiological Support Voo sound works well alongside other vagal toning practices: cold water exposure, gargling, humming, singing, gentle yoga, and social connection. You can also support your nervous system biochemically. GABA is the primary inhibitory neurotransmitter, the chemical signal that tells your brain to calm down. [Solgar GABA 500 mg (50 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000Z8WXMU?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers direct supplementation, though absorption is variable. Some people respond dramatically; others notice nothing. Aromatherapy can also support the practice. Lavender oil has documented anxiolytic effects and pairs well with breathwork. [Plant Therapy Lavender Essential Oil (30 mL)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0069SQQ2I?tag=spankyspinola-20) is steam-distilled and undiluted, appropriate for a diffuser or a drop on your wrists before you begin. The scent itself doesn't tone your vagus nerve, but it can create a sensory anchor, a signal to your nervous system that this is a space for settling. The most underrated support, though, is consistency. Your vagus nerve responds to pattern. A three-minute practice every morning for thirty days will rewire your baseline more effectively than an hour-long session once a month. The nervous system learns through repetition, not intensity. ## What Happens When You Feel the Anger Move There's a moment, sometimes in the third or fourth round of voo sound, when the vibration finds something. A tightness in your throat releases. Your eyes well up. A wave of heat moves through your chest. This is not a breakdown. This is your body completing a cycle it started days, weeks, or years ago. You might sob. You might feel a sudden surge of energy in your arms, the impulse to push or strike. You might feel nothing at all, just a quiet sense of space where there used to be compression. All of these are valid. The practice doesn't dictate what should happen. It creates conditions for your nervous system to do what it's been trying to do all along: process and release the charge. If you're working with old anger, anger that's been unfelt for a long time, this can be disorienting. You might not have words for what you're feeling. That's okay. The vagus nerve doesn't speak in sentences. It speaks in sensation, in breath, in the quality of your heartbeat. Let it speak. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### Can voo sound practice make my anger worse? No, but it can make you more aware of anger you were already carrying. If you've been suppressing your anger for years, vagal toning can increase your capacity to feel it, which might initially feel like an increase in anger itself. This is a sign the practice is working. Your nervous system is gaining the stability to process what it previously had to keep locked down. If the intensity feels unmanageable, work with a somatic therapist or explore the frameworks on [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) for additional support. ### How long before I notice a difference in my anger response? Most people notice some shift in their immediate physiology within the first session - deeper breath, lower heart rate, reduced muscle tension. Changes in your anger reactivity patterns typically emerge after two to three weeks of daily practice. You might notice you have a longer pause between trigger and reaction, or that you can name your anger without immediately escalating. These are signs of improved vagal tone. The deeper rewiring, the kind that changes your baseline nervous system state, unfolds over months. ### Is there anyone who shouldn't do voo sound practice? If you have active vocal cord pathology, recent throat surgery, or severe PTSD with dissociative symptoms, work with a professional before beginning. For most people, voo sound is safe and accessible. If you feel dizzy or panicky during the practice, you're likely breathing too forcefully or holding tension in your throat. Slow down, soften your effort, and let the sound be easy. The practice should feel grounding, not destabilizing. If it consistently doesn't, that's information worth exploring with support. ## The Long Game of Nervous System Rewiring Voo sound won't fix a toxic workplace. It won't repair a relationship built on dismissal. It won't dismantle the systemic conditions that generate justified rage. What it will do is give you a body that can stay present with all of that without shutting down or burning out. This is the work underneath the work. Before you can set a boundary, your nervous system has to believe it's safe to do so. Before you can speak your anger clearly, your body has to trust that it won't be annihilated for doing so. Vagal toning creates that trust, one three-minute session at a time. You're not escaping your anger. You're building the capacity to be with it fully, to let it inform you, to act from it with precision. That's a different kind of power, and it starts with a sound as simple as voo. ## Keep reading - [The Physical Practice of Rage: Movement, Sound, and Breath](/article/the-physical-practice-of-rage-movement-sound-and-breath) - [the assessments hub](/assessments) - [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on voo sound practice for vagal reset, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
